title: Telemachus versus Mentor
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Donן¿½t mind me, I beg you, old fellow, Iן¿½ll do very well here alone;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You must not be kept from your ן¿½Germanן¿½ because Iן¿½ve dropped in like a stone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leave all ceremony behind you, leave all thought of aught but yourself;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And leave, if you like, the Madeira, and a dozen cigars on the shelf.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As for me, you will say to your hostess well, I scarcely need give you a cue.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Chant my praise! All will list to Apollo, though Mercury pipe to a few.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Say just what you please, my dear boy; thereן¿½s more eloquence lies in youthן¿½s rash
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Outspoken heart-impulse than ever growled under this grizzling mustache.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Go, don the dress coat of our tyrant, youthן¿½s panoplied armor for fight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And tie the white neckcloth that rumples, like pleasure, and lasts but a night;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And pray the Nine Gods to avert you what time the Three Sisters shall frown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And youן¿½ll lose your high-comedy figure, and sit more at ease in your gown.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s off! Thereן¿½s his foot on the staircase. By Jove, what a bound! Really now
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did I ever leap like this springald, with Loveן¿½s chaplet green on my brow?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was I such an ass? No, I fancy. Indeed, I remember quite plain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A gravity mixed with my transports, a cheerfulness softened my pain.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s gone! Thereן¿½s the slam of his cab door, thereן¿½s the clatter of hoofs and the wheels;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And while he the light toe is tripping, in this armchair Iן¿½ll tilt up my heels.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s gone, and for what? For a tremor from a waist like a teetotum spun;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For a rosebud thatן¿½s crumpled by many before it is gathered by one.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is there naught in the halo of youth but the glow of a passionate race
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Midst the cheers and applause of a crowd to the goal of a beautiful face?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A race that is not to the swift, a prize that no merits enforce,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But is won by some faineant youth, who shall simply walk over the course?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Poor boy! shall I shock his conceit? When he talks of her cheekן¿½s loveliness,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall I say ן¿½twas the air of the room, and was due to carbonic excess?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That when waltzing she drooped on his breast, and the veins of her eyelids grew dim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas oxygenן¿½s absence she felt, but never the presence of him?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall I tell him first love is a fraud, a weakling thatן¿½s strangled in birth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Recalled with perfunctory tears, but lost in unsanctified mirth?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or shall I go bid him believe in all womankindן¿½s charm, and forget
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the light ringing laugh of the world the rattlesnakeן¿½s gay castanet?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall I tear out a leaf from my heart, from that book that forever is shut
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the past? Shall I speak of my first love Augusta my Lalage? But
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I forget. Was it really Augusta? No. ן¿½Twas Lucy! No. Mary! No. Di!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Never mind! they were all first and faithless, and yet Iן¿½ve forgotten just why.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No, no! Let him dream on and ever. Alas! he will waken too soon;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And it doesnן¿½t look well for October to always be preaching at June.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Poor boy! All his fond foolish trophies pinned yonder a bow from her hair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A few billets-doux, invitations, and whatן¿½s this? My name, I declare!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Humph! ן¿½Youן¿½ll come, for Iן¿½ve got you a prize, with beauty and money no end:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You know her, I think; ן¿½twas on dit she once was engaged to your friend;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But she says thatן¿½s all over.ן¿½ Ah, is it? Sweet Ethel! incomparable maid!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or what if the thing were a trick? this letter so freely displayed!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My opportune presence! No! nonsense! Will nobody answer the bell?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Call a cab! Half past ten. Not too late yet. Oh, Ethel! Why donן¿½t you go? Well?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Master said you would waitן¿½ Hang your master! ן¿½Have I ever a message to send?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yes, tell him Iן¿½ve gone to the German to dance with the friend of his friend.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Bret Harte (Francis) delivers a powerful performance in "Telemachus versus Mentor"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
